relationships, intellectual property they already own, etc etc etc and multiply themselves beyond geographic boundaries in ways other than go opening company stores or company outlets. For some franchising and five years ago I used to like talk everybody out of this. Lately I've been talking people into it. Situations change, the tendency of the the customer, the business owner, the professional practitioner to want things to want things done for them more than they want to learn things really begs. If you have a really successful system method and you have the ability to deliver on it it really begs the franchise formula where you are sharing in success. If you're gonna have to do a lot of stuff for them anyway the logic is you might as well get paid for it and the barriers, the difficulties legal and otherwise have diminished over the years. So there's that. Then there's distribution of various and sundry kinds that you also ought to try and think about. John's created a kind of distribution in what he described this morning. There's, well Chris Tomshack is franchised in chiropractic. What are you up to now? What's the number? 250 even? Sounds like a bullshit number. You should have said 249. So 250 franchised chiropractic practice clinics in how long? Three and a half years from Zip. But many of them now have or are having installed a second franchise which is weight loss which also has product. So now the franchise network is also a distributor network for product on which there's profit made every time somebody sells something every time somebody eats something and comes back and gets some more. So a lot of us don't think about distribution. We think about doing it all ourselves but really a distribution network can change everything. Media and manual labor. Media has a game-changers really really obvious right and so everybody's sort of been through that evolution. We can replace humans with robo calls, with webinars, with tele-seminars. We figured out we can fake the live tele-seminar or fake the live webinar and really replay it. I don't know how many of you guys know Rowletters but TJ is one of my favorite people but they're simple folk and this is some years ago but when people were first starting to figure out that the consumer couldn't tell the difference whether it's live or it's not live if it sounds live it is live and they have exactly the same experience right so he figured out you could rebroadcast tele-seminars and not actually do them live every Friday. That was a big sort of revolutionary idea. Unfortunately his first live tele-seminar that wasn't had in the middle of it we need to stop and turn this tape over which sort of gave it away but I don't think it affected anything but it sort of gave it away. It was funny after the fact but so everybody sort of figured this out right to some extent of how we can replace our own manual labor or others in various ways with media. The thing I would say about it is you should keep thinking about it all the time because these things hook together so like switching from a cold sale to a prepared sale ever it's example that's a that's a piece of media there doing work that without it a salesperson has to do every time again and again and again and against more resistance so everybody sort of realizes place manual labor with media and with tools especially repetitive manual labor that's good but you ought to continually think
